characters.relation: Lover-teacher who tried to rein him
characters.blurb: Sought Alcibiades’s soul more than his beauty; saved his life in battle and stood as the one man he truly feared to shame.
Wealthy dove who opposed the Sicilian dream. Ostracism nearly took one of them; Hyperbolus fell instead—and Sicily ruined both.
Kinsman who raised Alcibiades after Clinias’s death—the standard of glory the ward both imitated and outraged.
Died at Coronea; his fame and Alcmaeonid ties left Alcibiades rich, brilliant, and dangerously unsupervised.
Daughter of Hipponicus; tried to leave Alcibiades over his courtesans, then was fetched home from the archon’s office.
Torch-bearer of the Mysteries whose wealth and daughter tied Alcibiades to the first houses of Athens.
Heir of Hipponicus; tangled with Alcibiades in money, marriage, and the gossip of high Athens.

characters.relation: Persian satrap; final host and killer
characters.blurb: Phrygian satrap who alternately helped and hunted him; Lysander finally had him arrange Alcibiades’s murder.
Persian governor in Asia whom Alcibiades dazzled and advised—playing Persia against Sparta for Athens’s sake (and his own).
Navarch who closed the war at Aegospotami and would not leave Alcibiades alive to threaten the new order.
Blunt soldier who wanted to attack Syracuse at once; died early, leaving Nicias alone with a war he hated.
Arrived to stiffen Sicily against Athens; his skill sealed the disaster Alcibiades had sold to the demos.

characters.relation: Orator tangled in the Mysteries scandal
characters.blurb: Named in the Hermae/Mysteries panic that exploded as the Sicilian fleet sailed—the storm that drove Alcibiades to flee.
Tried to ostracize Alcibiades or Nicias; they combined and ostracized him instead—the last such vote.
Third man in the political fight that ended Hyperbolus; polished speaker without Alcibiades’s force.
Host at Sparta whose wife Timaea was said to bear Alcibiades a child—personal hate atop policy.
Queen whispered to have loved Alcibiades; the scandal sharpened Agis’s enmity.
General who opposed recalling Alcibiades; later murdered after playing all sides in the 411 revolution.
Helped bring Alcibiades back to the fleet; later restorer of democracy at Athens.
Shared commands in the Ionian and Hellespontine campaigns of Alcibiades’s second Athenian career.
Fell when Alcibiades and the Athenian captains destroyed his fleet—the peak of the recall years.
Left in charge against orders, he provoked Lysander and lost ships—the excuse Athens used to cashier Alcibiades again.
Courtesan who was with him in Phrygia and wrapped his body after the assassins burned him out.
Commander entangled in the Persian–Spartan deals Alcibiades tried to manage through Tisaphernes.
Appears among the clever, dangerous young men of Alcibiades’s set—foreshadowing the Thirty.
Relative who defended Alcibiades’s interests in the assembly during the recalls and accusations.

Ward of Pericles whose beauty, wealth, and audacity made him the most desired and most dangerous man in Athens. He won great victories, betrayed every city that trusted him, and died by assassins' fire in a Phrygian village—with only Timandra to wrap his body.

characters.relation: Oligarch and rival in the revolution
characters.blurb: Played all sides in the 411 coup and its collapse; his moves against Alcibiades and Phrynichus illustrate the lethal politics of the age of the Thirty.
Wealthy tanner-politician who suffered Alcibiades's contemptuous treatment in youth—Plutarch tells the story of wine thrown in his face, which Anytus's admirers found charming rather than insulting.

The famous Athenian misanthrope who despised all mankind except Alcibiades—because, he said, the young man would one day cause great calamity to Athens. Plutarch records the prophecy with evident irony.
Popular leader who stoked the Mysteries panic to drive Alcibiades from the city before the Sicilian fleet sailed; he was himself later killed in the political violence that followed.
Pericles's brother who shared legal guardianship of Alcibiades and Clinias after their father's death—but it was Pericles's household that shaped the boy.

characters.relation: Philosopher who wrote him into the dialogues
characters.blurb: Wrote the Symposium and the two Alcibiades dialogues; his portrait of the brilliant, vain, and beloved young man is the deepest literary account Plutarch cites.

Judged Alcibiades's conduct of the war brilliantly but his private life disastrously; his cool assessment of the Sicilian expedition as genius undone by personal scandal stands behind Plutarch's account.
In the Frogs and elsewhere Aristophanes caught Athens's ambivalence about Alcibiades—that the city both longed for him and feared him, missing him as it damned him.
Brilliant speechwriter involved in the Four Hundred's revolution; named among the clever, dangerous figures whose careers intersect Alcibiades's last Athenian years.
An Athenian who gave Alcibiades money to buy a horse at Olympia; Alcibiades entered it under his own name instead—a piece of shameless appropriation Plutarch records without softening.
The Hermae—herm-pillars of Hermes—were mutilated on the eve of the Sicilian fleet's departure; the sacrilege was blamed on Alcibiades's circle and ignited the Mysteries panic.
The Eleusinian Mysteries of Demeter and Persephone were said to have been parodied at Alcibiades's private parties—the charge that made him a target for prosecution even as the fleet sailed.

characters.relation: Priestess who refused to curse him
characters.blurb: When the assembly ordered Athenian priests to curse Alcibiades formally, Theano alone refused, saying she was a priestess to pray, not to curse—a moment of quiet dignity Plutarch admires.

characters.relation: Wealthy host of the Mysteries parody
characters.blurb: Named alongside others at whose houses the sacred Eleusinian rites were reportedly mocked—the social network of the scandal that drove Alcibiades to defect to Sparta.

characters.relation: Poet Alcibiades sought from a schoolmaster
characters.blurb: When a teacher said he had no Homer, Alcibiades struck him—Plutarch uses the story to show the young man's ferocious expectations of learning.

characters.relation: Tragedian cited for the Sicilian glory
characters.blurb: Euripides's account of the Sicilian spectacle is invoked by Plutarch alongside Thucydides when describing the splendor of the expedition Alcibiades championed.

characters.relation: Herald in the Mysteries parody; later refused to curse
characters.blurb: Played herald in the private Mysteries parody; afterward, when the priesthood was ordered to curse Alcibiades, Theodorus of Phegaea alone refused, saying he was a priest to pray, not to curse.
Daughter of Megacles; through her Alcibiades traced his lineage to the Alcmaeonid house of Athens.
Father of Dinomache; head of the great Alcmaeonid clan that gave Alcibiades his most illustrious blood-line.
Spartan woman who nursed Alcibiades—one of the few people around him Plutarch considers worthy of a name.
Attendant who taught the young Alcibiades; Plato records his name, one detail of the boy's pampered upbringing.
Socratic writer who noted the name of Alcibiades's nurse—cited by Plutarch to show what fame a great subject's childhood attracts.

characters.relation: Athenian general of the same era
characters.blurb: The general, not the orator: listed alongside Nicias, Lamachus, Phormion, and Thrasybulus as an illustrious contemporary whose mother's name no one troubled to record.
Distinguished Athenian admiral mentioned in Plutarch's opening to highlight how Socrates's friendship uniquely preserved even domestic details of Alcibiades's life.

characters.relation: Comic poet who mocked Alcibiades's son
characters.blurb: Athenian comedian who ridiculed Alcibiades's son for imitating his father's trailing robe and lisping speech—evidence the affectations became a city-wide joke.

characters.relation: Man Alcibiades ran to as a boy
characters.blurb: An Athenian admirer in whose house the young Alcibiades once took refuge during a quarrel, prompting Ariphron to consider a public search.
Stoic thinker whose remark—that he held his beloved only by the ears—Plutarch uses to illustrate how Socrates alone reached Alcibiades through reason, not pleasure.
Named alongside Dioclides as one of the informers whose accusations drove the city's rage during the Herms and Mysteries scandal as the Sicilian fleet sailed.
Deposed in the panic over the mutilation of the Hermae; later shown to have lied (the moon was new when he claimed to see by moonlight), but fear made Athens believe him.

characters.relation: Comic poet; witness to the Mysteries panic
characters.blurb: The comic playwright (distinct from the oligarchic general Phrynichus) who mocked Dioclides and Teucer in verse—evidence of how Athens's comic stage shadowed every crisis.

characters.relation: Prisoner who persuaded Andocides to confess
characters.blurb: A fellow detainee of lesser repute but remarkable cunning who convinced Andocides that confessing and naming others, however falsely, was the only safe path out of prison.
Attic chronicler Plutarch cites for tracing Andocides's lineage back to Ulysses—an example of the obscure detective work ancient genealogists applied to the famous.

characters.relation: Son of Cimon; formal accuser of Alcibiades
characters.blurb: Filed the official information that charged Alcibiades with profaning the Mysteries—the legal document Plutarch quotes verbatim, naming himself as plaintiff.

characters.relation: Persian king Alcibiades hoped to serve
characters.blurb: The Great King whose court Alcibiades intended to reach from Bithynia, believing his services against enemies of Persia would find a better welcome than Themistocles's.
The only Athenian commander to save his squadron—eight galleys—when Lysander destroyed the fleet; his escape became a footnote to the catastrophe Alcibiades had warned against.

characters.relation: General at Aegospotami who dismissed Alcibiades
characters.blurb: One of the three successors who replaced Alcibiades; his contemptuous order to leave the camp sealed the Athenians' fate when Lysander struck.
Posted at Aegospotami with Tydeus and Adimantus; their careless anchorage gave Lysander the opening that ended Athenian sea power.
Third of the new-made commanders who ignored Alcibiades's warning at Aegospotami; later suspected of treason by some accounts.
Called the Corinthian, though born in Hyccara in Sicily; said to be the daughter of Timandra who wrapped Alcibiades's body—a story that ties the most celebrated hetaera to his end.

characters.relation: Pharnabazus's brother; carried out the murder
characters.blurb: Pharnabazus delegated the killing of Alcibiades to his brother Magaeus, who surrounded the house and set it on fire.

characters.relation: Pharnabazus's uncle; in the murder plot
characters.blurb: Joined Magaeus in carrying out Pharnabazus's order to kill Alcibiades; the two encircled the house with fire while he slept.

characters.relation: Pythian flute-victor at the triumphal return
characters.blurb: A Pythian games winner said by Duris (but no other source) to have played the flute to mark time for Alcibiades's oarsmen on the triumphal return to Athens.

characters.relation: Tragic actor at the triumphal return
characters.blurb: Celebrated tragedian whom Duris claims gave the rowing commands in stage costume; Plutarch doubts the story but records it as a sample of Duris's exaggeration.

characters.relation: Samian historian claiming descent from Alcibiades
characters.blurb: Wrote sensational accounts of Alcibiades's triumph—purple sails, famous flutists, theatrical trappings—which Theopompus, Ephorus, and Xenophon all contradict.
The Chian historian cited alongside Ephorus and Xenophon as sources that say nothing of the theatrical trappings Duris attributed to Alcibiades's triumphal return.
Major 4th-century historian of Greek affairs; listed with Theopompus and Xenophon as a sober source that contradicts Duris's colorful version of the triumphal return.
Athenian general and writer; Plutarch cites him alongside Theopompus and Ephorus as a reliable authority who records none of Duris's theatrical embellishments.

characters.relation: Athenian general; father of Thessalus the accuser
characters.blurb: Son of Miltiades and the great rival of Pericles; his son Thessalus filed the formal indictment charging Alcibiades with profaning the Mysteries—so Cimon's family supplied the decisive legal weapon against him.

characters.relation: Son born to Timaea; Alcibiades's alleged child by the Spartan queen
characters.blurb: Timaea named him Leotychides in public, but whispered 'Alcibiades' in private. King Agis, who had been away ten months before the birth, refused to acknowledge him; the boy was accordingly excluded from the Spartan royal succession.

Kept under lock and key in Alcibiades's house until he had painted the walls throughout, then dismissed with a handsome payment. Plutarch cites the episode as typical of Alcibiades's mixture of high-handedness and generosity.

characters.relation: Byzantine who betrayed the city to Alcibiades
characters.blurb: Opened Byzantine gates to Alcibiades together with Lycurgus and others, on his promise to spare the inhabitants' lives and property—ending the revolt without massacre and earning Alcibiades rare praise for clemency.

characters.relation: Byzantine who betrayed the city alongside Anaxilaus
characters.blurb: One of the Byzantines who arranged to hand over the city to Alcibiades in exchange for guarantees of life and property; distinct from the Spartan lawgiver of the same name.

characters.relation: Spartan lawgiver; model of Spartan virtue
characters.blurb: The legendary architect of Spartan discipline; cited in the Life to contrast the character Lycurgus designed to form with the reality of Alcibiades's conduct in Sparta.

Governor holding Chalcedon for Pharnabazus; made a sally against Alcibiades's besieging army at the same moment Pharnabazus arrived to relieve the town. Alcibiades divided his force and repulsed both simultaneously.

characters.relation: Athenian orator who proposed full powers for the Sicilian generals
characters.blurb: Moved that the generals be given absolute authority over preparations and the conduct of the whole Sicilian expedition—the decree that gave Alcibiades, Nicias, and Lamachus their sweeping mandate.

characters.relation: Commentator who said Greece could not bear a second Alcibiades
characters.blurb: Quoted by Plutarch for the remark that Greece was too small to support two Alcibiades—a wry verdict on the city's mixture of awe and exhaustion at the man's ambition.

Of the deme Agraule; his daughter Theano, alone among the priests and priestesses, refused the popular decree that the sacred ministers should formally curse Alcibiades, saying her office obliged her to prayers, not execrations.

characters.relation: Rival producer of games whom Alcibiades struck in public
characters.blurb: Mounted theatrical games in competition with Alcibiades; Alcibiades punched him in the face before the judges and the crowd—an act of brazen arrogance that the city, in its infatuation, apparently endured.
An Athenian citizen whose son Anytus became one of Alcibiades's many infatuated admirers—and who later, as the prosecutor of Socrates, played a fateful role in Athenian history.
Father of Phaeax, who with Nicias was one of the only men in Athens able to contest Alcibiades's influence with the people—a detail Plutarch records to measure the scale of Alcibiades's dominance.
Father of Nicias, Alcibiades's principal rival for popular favour; mentioned to identify the statesman who, with Phaeax, was the only other Athenian capable of competing with Alcibiades.
Father of the oligarch Critias, who later moved in the assembly the decree recalling Alcibiades from exile—a fact Plutarch documents from Critias's own elegies.
Father of Thrasybulus the democrat; noted because his son was the particular enemy of Alcibiades in the army, going especially to Athens to prosecute him after Notium.

characters.relation: Son of Ajax; legendary paternal ancestor of Alcibiades
characters.blurb: Son of the hero Ajax; through him Alcibiades traced his descent on his father Clinias's side to the Aeacid line—a pedigree that paired Homeric heroism with the Alcmaeonid distinction of his mother's family.

nameResolutions.note: Abstract reference to the Spartan lawgiver as a standard of Spartan character
The Byzantine citizen who betrayed the city to Alcibiades
